content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 17, 1998

Russell H. Miller
Law Office of Russell H. Miller
20 Park Road, Suite E
Burlingame, California 94010-4443

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-98-276

Dear Mr. Miller:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Clara County Supervisor S. Joseph Simitian regarding the ethics provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your request was prompted by our response to you in the Miller Advice Letter, No. A-98-229.

Since you have not provided all of the material facts, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(D).) Informal assistance does not provide the immunity conferred by formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

QUESTIONS

1. May Supervisor Simitian accept a scholarship to attend the graduate International Policy Studies program at Stanford University from a source other than the university?

2. Would the scholarship be treated as income or a gift?

3. Would the scholarship be treated as income or a gift if the supervisor provided a lecture, or series of lectures, on his course of study and its relationship to public service in return?

___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² You did not provide any information regarding the "foundation," the source of the scholarship.

File No. I-98-276
Page 2

4. Would the scholarship be treated as income or a gift if the scholarship was offered by
a public charitable 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The supervisor may not accept gifts from any single source if the value of the gift
exceeds the annual gift limit.

2. The scholarship would be a "gift" subject to the gift limit.

3. The scholarship would be a prohibited honorarium.

4. The scholarship would be a "gift" subject to the gift limit.

FACTS

Supervisor Simitian wants to attend a master's degree program in International Policy
Studies at Stanford University. The graduate program will provide the supervisor with training
in international aspects of public policy, business, political science and the economy.

In the Miller Advice Letter, No. A-98-229, the supervisor asked the Commission whether
he could use campaign funds to pay the tuition for the program. We advised that holding an
academic degree is not directly related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose and
therefore campaign funds could not be used for that purpose.

The supervisor would now like to know whether a third party other than the university
could pay his tuition instead.

ANALYSIS

In the June 1990 primary election, the voters enacted Proposition 112, a governmental
ethics reform measure placed on the ballot by the Legislature. Pursuant to Proposition 112, the
Legislature added a comprehensive ethics reform package to the Political Reform Act. (Stats.
1990, ch. 84.) In 1994, the Legislature amended the ethics provisions of the Act to include a ban
on the receipt of honoraria and a limit on the receipt of gifts by local elected officials. (Stats.
1994, ch. 36.)

As a local elected official, Supervisor Simitian is subject to the honoraria ban and the gift
limit set forth in the ethics provisions of the Act.

File No. I-98-276
Page 3

Gift Limit

The ethics provisions of the Act prohibit all public officials, including Supervisor
Simitian, from receiving gifts from any single source over a specified amount. (Section
89503(a).) The gift limit is currently $290 per year. Effective January 1, 1999, the gift limit will
be $300 per year. (Regulation 18940.2.) The Act defines a "gift" as:

"[A]ny payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient,
to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything
of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course
of business to members of the public without regard to official
status." (Section 82028(a), emphasis added.)

The emphasized language, "that confers a personal benefit on the recipient," was added to
section 82028 by the Legislature last year. (Stats. 1997, ch. 450.) You would like to know
whether a scholarship to attend the master's degree program at Stanford is a "gift." As we
advised in our previous letter to you, the benefit of holding a master's degree is essentially
personal. As such, the payment would "confer a personal benefit" on the supervisor.
Accordingly, the scholarship would be considered a gift unless a gift exception applies.

Exceptions to the term "Gift" and the Gift Limit

The ethics provisions of the Act include exceptions to the term "gift" and exceptions to
the gift limit. (Sections 82028(b); 89506(a).) You have inquired about payments from a public
charitable 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization. One exception to the gift limit applies to payments
from 501(c)(3) nonprofit organizations. (Section 89506(a)(2).) However, this exception applies
only to travel payments. It does not appear that an exception in the ethics provisions of the Act
would apply to your facts. Therefore, the scholarship would be considered a gift subject to the
gift limit.

Income or Prohibited Honoraria

You would like to know whether the scholarship may be treated as income, rather than as
a gift, if the supervisor provides lectures on his course of study and its relationship to public
service in return. Section 82030 defines "income" as a payment received including any salary,
wage, advance, gift, reimbursement for expenses and per diem. When an official claims that a
payment is not a gift because he or she provided consideration, the official has the burden of
proving that the consideration received is of equal or greater value. (Coulter Advice Letter,
No. A-98-089.) However, the receipt of a payment may be prohibited by the ethics provisions of
the Act when the payment would be considered an honorarium.

File No. I-98-276
Page 4

The ethics provisions of the Act prohibit local elected officials, such as Supervisor
Simitian, from accepting any honorarium. An "honorarium" includes "any
payment made in consideration for any speech given." (Section 89501(a).) "Honorarium" does
not include earned income for personal services that are customarily provided in connection with
the practice of a "bona fide business, trade or profession." (Section 89501(b); Regulation
18932.1.) A "bona fide business, trade or profession" does not include holding elective office.
Accordingly, the honoraria ban would prohibit the supervisor from providing a lecture to the
donor as consideration in exchange for a scholarship.

For future reference, I have enclosed a fact sheet that describes the gift and honoraria
laws contained in the ethics provisions of the Act. If you have any other questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
